abstract.text: This article describes the KeyboardKit localization engine.

inlineContent.text: A flexible localization engine is an important part of a keyboard, where every supported locale should be able to localize the keyboard and its texts, layout, and secondary callout actions.
In KeyboardKit, all
locales define localized strings, assets, and locale-specific information. KeyboardKit also extends the native
type with additional capabilities.
unlocks localized keyboards and services for all
locales in your license. Information about Pro features can be found further down.
KeyboardKit extends
and uses it as a namespace for locale-related types and views, like the locale-specific
KeyboardKit supports
keyboard
s, like
, and
, as defined by
KeyboardKit extends
with localized strings that can be translated with
, as well as collection modifiers, locale-specific properties like
inforamation, search & sorting capabilities, and much more.
See the native
type’s documentation for a complete list of extensions that are applied to the type by KeyboardKit.
KeyboardKit extends
with locale-specific views, that can be used to perform native locale operations, pick locales, etc.
The
-specific
can be used to change the
by tapping or long pressing any view that it’s applied to.
You can apply the menu to any view with the
view modifier.
automatically applies it to every key that triggers the
action, as well as to the space bar if the

unlocks more

and a
for every locale in your license.
This lets KeyboardKit create a fully localized
for every locale, with no additional code needed. You can customize any part for any locale at any time, whenever needed.
After setting up KeyboardKit Pro with your license key, as described in the
article, you can just change the
to automatically update the keyboard view for any locale in your license.
The
can be used to get and set the current
and the available
. You can also use the context’s
to set which of the available locales to explicitly add to the keyboard.
If
or the
’s added locales has multiple values, you can select the next locale with
or let the user select locales with a
You can automatically add a context menu to the keyboard by inserting a
button, or add a context menu to any view with the
view modifier.
Setting the
will update the controller’s
primaryLanguage
, which controls things like spell checking and text direction. This also sets the keyboard’s language subtitle in the keyboard switcher.
The
property seems to always return
, even after it has been set properly (and works). This can be a bit confusing, but just check that the proper language is displayed in the system keyboard switcher.
KeyboardKit supports LTR (Left-To-Right) & RTL (Right-To-Left) locales if you use the
. Just change the locale as explained above, and KeyboardKit will automatically adjust the text direction, spell checking, etc.
If you want to use a single RTL
without using KeyboardKit, you can adjust the
Info.plist
file in your keyboard extension like this:
Set
PrefersRightToLeft
to
Set
PrimaryLanguage
to the language code of your locale, e.g.
fa
for Perian (Farsi).
Just be aware that setting the primary language like this may affect external keyboard mappings if you don’t set the primary language.
Each
locale has a localized file in
Resources/[id].lproj
with texts that are applied with the
enum.
For instance, this translates the numeric button key for the current locale:
To translate the same text for a certain
, you can use the various
functions, which take a
parameter value:
Besides localized strings, you can use the context
to set how locales are translated for a keyboard.
If you need a locale that KeyboardKit doesn’t support yet, like Japanese or Simplified Chinese, you can implement a custom
configuration to support it, or reach out to add support for a new
To fully support a certain
, you must add locale-specific
and
implementations, and create a localization folder in
. You may also have to provide custom autocoplete logic.

If you have expertise on a certain locale that you want KeyboardKit to support, you can reach out to help adding it to KeyboardKit Pro. If a locale gets added as a result of your contribution, you will get a free Basic license or a discount on the higher plans.
